title: NGINX Security Headers: The Complete Guide to Hardening Your Web Server
description: A comprehensive guide to configuring HTTP security headers in NGINX — covering HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Permissions-Policy, Referrer-Policy, and the critical add_header inheritance caveat that breaks most configurations. Includes production-ready snippets, testing tools, and real-world scenarios for DevOps engineers.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: NGINX Security Headers shield illustration showing HTTP security headers protecting a web server

sections.title: 1. Introduction
sections.description: Why security headers matter and what this guide covers.
content: HTTP security headers are response headers that instruct browsers how to behave when rendering your website. They are your first line of defense against common web attacks — cross-site scripting (XSS), clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, protocol downgrade attacks, and data injection.

The critical insight: security headers are cheap to implement, have zero performance impact, and close real attack vectors. Yet they are surprisingly easy to misconfigure in NGINX due to the add_header inheritance behavior.

This guide covers every security header you should configure in NGINX, explains what each one does, provides production-ready configuration snippets, reveals the #1 NGINX security header pitfall (and how to avoid it), and includes testing tools to verify your setup.

infoBox.content: NGINX version 1.14+ is recommended for full security header support. Nearly all distributions package this or newer.

cards.title: What You Will Learn
cards.desc: Every major HTTP security header, how to configure them in NGINX, the add_header inheritance trap, production snippets, and testing methodologies.

cards.title: Prerequisites
cards.desc: A running NGINX server (any OS), basic familiarity with nginx.conf structure, and a browser with DevTools for testing.

sections.title: 2. What Are HTTP Security Headers?
sections.description: Understanding the role of response headers in browser security.
content: When a browser sends an HTTP request to your NGINX server, the server responds with both the requested content and a set of HTTP headers. Security headers are specific response headers that tell the browser to enforce certain security policies.

Without security headers, browsers use default behavior — which is often insecure. For example, without X-Content-Type-Options, a browser might sniff the content of a JavaScript file and execute it even if the server declared it as CSS. Without X-Frame-Options, an attacker can embed your site in an iframe and trick users into clicking buttons they cannot see (clickjacking).

Think of security headers as a contract between your server and the browser: 'Here are the rules for how to handle my content.' Browsers that support these headers will enforce them. Older browsers ignore them, which is harmless.

items.label: The 7 Essential Security Headers at a Glance

rows: Script injection via disguised file types

sections.title: 3. The Critical NGINX Caveat: add_header Inheritance
sections.description: The #1 mistake that silently breaks security headers in NGINX.
content: This is the most important section in this guide. The NGINX add_header directive has a behavior that causes countless security misconfigurations: when you use add_header in a nested block (like a location block), it completely replaces all add_header directives from parent blocks.
It does NOT merge them. It does NOT inherit them. It replaces them entirely.
This means if you define security headers in your server block and then add any add_header in a location block, all server-level headers are lost for that location.

infoBox.content: This is the #1 cause of missing security headers in production. A 2024 scan of the top 10,000 websites found that ~30% had gaps caused by this exact issue.

items.label: The Problem: What Happens
Three Solutions to the Inheritance Problem
text: Solution 1: NEVER use add_header in location blocks — keep them all at the server level or an included snippet.
Solution 2: If you MUST have location-level add_header, repeat ALL security headers in that location block.
Solution 3: Use a shared include file for all headers and include it in every block that needs them.

items.label: Solution 1: No Location-Level add_header
Solution 2: Repeat Headers in Location (if you must)
Solution 3: Shared Include Snippet (Recommended)

sections.title: 4. Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)
sections.description: Forcing HTTPS connections and preventing SSL stripping attacks.
content: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) tells browsers to ALWAYS connect to your site over HTTPS — even if the user types http:// or clicks a non-HTTPS link. Once a browser sees this header, it refuses to make plain HTTP connections to your domain for the specified duration.

This is one of the most impactful security headers you can set, and one of the most consequential to get wrong. If you enable HSTS with includeSubDomains and a subdomain does not support HTTPS, that subdomain becomes permanently unreachable for the duration of the max-age.

items.label: Basic HSTS Configuration
items.code:
add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" always;

# Parameters:
# max-age       — seconds the browser should remember (31536000 = 1 year)
# includeSubDomains — apply to all subdomains
# preload        — allow inclusion in browser preload lists (optional)
# always        — send even on error responses (4xx/5xx)

items.label: HSTS Preload (Advanced)
items.text:
The preload directive allows your site to be included in browser preload lists. Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge ship with a hardcoded list of HSTS sites. Once preloaded, your site is HSTS-enabled from the very first visit — no initial HTTP connection is ever made.

WARNING: HSTS preload is effectively PERMANENT. Removal requests must be made to each browser vendor and may take months to propagate. Do NOT enable preload unless you are certain every current and future subdomain will support HTTPS forever.

items.label: HSTS Preload Configuration
The 'always' Parameter — Why It Matters
content: Without the always parameter, NGINX only sends add_header directives on successful responses (2xx and 3xx). This means 404, 500, 502, 503, and 429 (rate limit) responses will NOT carry the HSTS header.
If a browser receives a 404 without HSTS, and this happens during an initial HTTP connection, the browser proceeds over HTTP — defeating the purpose of HSTS. Always add the 'always' parameter.

items.label: HSTS Deployment Phases (Recommended)
text: Phase 1 — Test: Set max-age to a low value (e.g., 300 = 5 minutes) and monitor for issues.
Phase 2 — Gradual: If no issues after a week, increase to max-age=31536000 (1 year) with includeSubDomains.
Phase 3 — Preload (optional): After months of stable HTTPS, consider submitting to HSTS preload list.

sections.title: 5. X-Frame-Options
sections.description: Preventing clickjacking attacks by controlling iframe embedding.
content: X-Frame-Options protects your users from clickjacking attacks. Clickjacking works by embedding your site in a transparent iframe on an attacker's page. When the user clicks what they think is a button on the attacker's site, they are actually clicking a button on your site — potentially authorizing a transaction, changing settings, or deleting an account.
This header tells the browser whether your page can be displayed in an iframe, frame, object, or embed element.

items.label: X-Frame-Options Configuration
When to Use DENY vs SAMEORIGIN
text: DENY: Use when your site should NEVER be embedded in any iframe. This is the most secure option. Suitable for admin panels, login pages, payment portals, and banking sites.
SAMEORIGIN: Use when your own site embeds itself in iframes (e.g., a dashboard with embedded widgets). This is the recommended default for most websites.

Note: If you need to allow embedding from specific external domains, you MUST use the Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive instead. X-Frame-Options ALLOW-FROM is deprecated and not consistently supported.

items.label: Modern Alternative: CSP frame-ancestors

sections.title: 6. X-Content-Type-Options
sections.description: Preventing MIME-type sniffing attacks.
content: Browsers have a feature called MIME sniffing or content type sniffing. When a server sends a file, the browser may ignore the declared Content-Type and try to guess the actual type by examining the file content. This was designed for older websites that misconfigured MIME types.

Attackers exploit this by uploading a file that claims to be an image (Content-Type: image/jpeg) but contains JavaScript. If the browser sniffs it and executes it as JavaScript, the attacker has achieved cross-site scripting.
Setting X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff instructs the browser to ALWAYS trust the declared Content-Type and never sniff the content.

items.label: X-Content-Type-Options Configuration

sections.title: 7. Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
sections.description: The most powerful security header — preventing XSS and data injection attacks.
content: Content-Security-Policy (CSP) is the single most powerful security header. It defines exactly which sources of content the browser is allowed to load: scripts, styles, images, fonts, media, frames, and more. Any resource from a source not listed in the policy is blocked by the browser.

CSP is your primary defense against Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks. Even if an attacker manages to inject a script tag into your page, the browser will refuse to execute it unless the script's source is explicitly allowed by your CSP.

CSP is also complex to configure correctly. A too-restrictive policy breaks your site. A too-permissive policy provides no protection. Always start with a strict policy and relax it only when necessary.

infoBox.content: Always start with Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only mode first. This sends violations to a report endpoint without actually blocking anything. Monitor the reports, adjust your policy, and switch to enforcement mode only after you are confident.

rows: Who can embed your site in iframes
Trust scripts loaded by trusted scripts

items.label: The 'unsafe-inline' Problem
items.text: The most common CSP dilemma: inline scripts and styles are blocked by default. If your application uses inline event handlers (onclick, onload), inline <script> blocks, or inline <style> tags — as most do — you need either 'unsafe-inline' (which weakens protection) or a nonce/hash-based approach (which is more secure but requires server-side integration).

items.label: Strict CSP (Recommended Starting Point)
Moderate CSP (Most Common Pattern)
Permissive CSP (for Sites with Many Third-Party Resources)
CSP Report-Only Mode (Safe Testing)
CSP with Nonces (Advanced — Most Secure)
items.text: Instead of using 'unsafe-inline', you can generate a cryptographically random nonce (number used once) for each request and add it to your script tags. The server includes the same nonce in the CSP header. Only scripts with the matching nonce will execute.
items.contentAfter: This approach requires server-side integration: your application must generate a unique nonce per request, include it in the CSP header, and add it to every script tag. NGINX alone cannot generate nonces — you need your application to do it.

sections.title: 8. Referrer-Policy
sections.description: Controlling what information is sent in the Referer header.
content: When a user clicks a link on your page, the browser sends a Referer (note: the actual header misspells it as Referer) header to the destination site. This header reveals the URL the user came from. For sensitive pages (password reset, payment page, admin panel), this can leak private information.
Referrer-Policy controls how much information is included in the Referer header when navigating away from your site.

rows: Reveal domain but not specific page
Full URL same-origin, origin only cross-origin
Send Referer only for same-origin requests
Full URL same-origin, origin only HTTPS→HTTPS, nothing to downgrade

items.label: Recommended Configuration

sections.title: 9. Permissions-Policy (Formerly Feature-Policy)
sections.description: Restricting browser features like camera, microphone, and geolocation.
content: Permissions-Policy (formerly Feature-Policy) allows you to control which browser features your site and its embedded third-party content can use. This prevents malicious scripts from accessing sensitive device capabilities like the camera, microphone, geolocation, and accelerometer without user consent.
This header is particularly important for sites that embed third-party content (ads, analytics, social widgets) — it restricts what those third parties can access.

items.label: Permissions-Policy Configuration
items.code:
# Restrict all sensitive features (recommended default)
add_header Permissions-Policy "
    camera=(),
    microphone=(),
    geolocation=(),
    payment=(),
    usb=(),
    accelerometer=(),
    gyroscope=(),
    magnetometer=(),
    midi=(),
    sync-xhr=(),
    display-capture=()
" always;

items.label: Allowing Specific Origins
items.code:
# Allow specific features for specific origins
add_header Permissions-Policy "
    camera=(self "https://zoom.example.com"),
    microphone=(self "https://zoom.example.com"),
    geolocation=(self),
    payment=(),
    accelerometer=()
" always;

sections.title: 10. X-XSS-Protection
sections.description: Enabling legacy browser cross-site scripting filters.
content: X-XSS-Protection controls the built-in XSS filter in older browsers (Internet Explorer, legacy Edge, older Safari). Modern browsers have deprecated this filter in favor of Content-Security-Policy, but setting this header provides defense-in-depth for users on legacy browsers.
Note: In Chrome 78+ and modern browsers, the XSS filter is removed entirely. This header is only for legacy compatibility.

items.label: X-XSS-Protection Options
Recommendation
items.text: Set X-XSS-Protection to "1; mode=block" for legacy browser compatibility, combined with a strong Content-Security-Policy for modern browsers. If you do not need legacy support, set it to "0" and rely entirely on CSP.

sections.title: 11. Server Tokens (Server Header)
sections.description: Hiding NGINX version information from attackers.
content: By default, NGINX includes its version number in the Server response header (e.g., Server: nginx/1.24.0). This gives attackers information about your server — if a known vulnerability exists in your version, you have made their reconnaissance easier.
Setting server_tokens off removes the version number, showing only 'nginx' (e.g., Server: nginx).

items.label: Server Tokens Configuration
items.code:
# Hide NGINX version
server_tokens off;

# Result:
# Before: Server: nginx/1.24.0
# After:  Server: nginx

items.label: Completely Remove Server Header (Advanced)
items.text: To completely remove the Server header, you need the ngx_headers_more module (third-party) or NGINX Plus. NGINX Open Source does not support removing the Server header entirely — only hiding the version.
items.code:
# With ngx_headers_more module:
more_clear_headers "Server";

# Result:
# Server header is removed entirely

items.label: Complete Security Headers File

sections.title: 13. How to Use the Snippet in Your Server Blocks
sections.description: Proper include patterns that avoid the inheritance trap.

items.label: Method 1: Include in Every Server Block (Recommended)
Method 2: Include Globally in nginx.conf
Method 3: Include in Each HTTP and HTTPS Server Block

sections.title: 14. Testing and Verifying Security Headers
sections.description: How to verify your security headers are properly configured.

items.label: Using curl (Command Line)
items.code:
# Check all response headers
user@server:~$ curl -I https://example.com
HTTP/2 200
server: nginx
content-type: text/html
strict-transport-security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload
x-frame-options: SAMEORIGIN
x-content-type-options: nosniff
x-xss-protection: 1; mode=block
referrer-policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
permissions-policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()
content-security-policy: default-src 'self'...

# Check specific header
user@server:~$ curl -s -D- https://example.com | grep -i strict-transport
strict-transport-security: max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload

items.label: Online Testing Tools
text: Security Headers (securityheaders.com) — Scan your site and get a letter grade (A+ to F). The industry standard for security header verification.
Mozilla Observatory (observatory.mozilla.org) — Comprehensive scan including TLS, headers, and more.
HSTS Preload Checker (hstspreload.org) — Verify if your domain is eligible for HSTS preloading.
CSP Evaluator (csp-evaluator.withgoogle.com) — Google's tool for evaluating CSP quality.

items.label: Browser DevTools
items.text:
Open Chrome DevTools (F12) → Network tab → Click a request → Check the Response Headers section. All security headers should appear here.

Pro tip: Filter by 'Doc' to see only HTML responses. Check that security headers are present on: the main page, error pages (404, 500), and API responses.

items.label: Automated Testing Script
items.code:
#!/bin/bash
# security-headers-check.sh — Test script

URL="${1:-https://example.com}"

echo "=== Testing Security Headers for $URL ==="
echo ""

# Get response headers
HEADERS=$(curl -sI "$URL")

# Check each header
echo "1. Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS):"
echo "$HEADERS" | grep -i "strict-transport-security"
echo ""

echo "2. X-Frame-Options:"
echo "$HEADERS" | grep -i "x-frame-options"
echo ""

echo "3. X-Content-Type-Options:"
echo "$HEADERS" | grep -i "x-content-type-options"
echo ""

echo "4. Content-Security-Policy:"
echo "$HEADERS" | grep -i "content-security-policy"
echo ""

echo "5. Referrer-Policy:"
echo "$HEADERS" | grep -i "referrer-policy"
echo ""

echo "6. Permissions-Policy:"
echo "$HEADERS" | grep -i "permissions-policy"
echo ""

echo "7. Server header (should not show version):"
echo "$HEADERS" | grep -i "server"
echo ""

# Check HSTS on 404
NOT_FOUND_HEADERS=$(curl -sI "$URL/nonexistent-page-12345")
echo "=== HSTS on 404 page (must have 'always' parameter) ==="
echo "$NOT_FOUND_HEADERS" | grep -i "strict-transport-security"
echo ""

# Print grade
echo "=== Check online ==="
echo "Visit: https://securityheaders.com/?q=$URL"
echo "Visit: https://observatory.mozilla.org/analyze/$URL"

sections.title: 15. Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
sections.description: Frequent security header misconfigurations and their solutions.

items.label: Mistake 1: Missing 'always' Parameter
items.content: Without the always parameter, security headers are only sent on 2xx and 3xx responses. Error pages (4xx, 5xx) do not get headers. This means a 404 response does not carry HSTS, and a browser that encounters a 404 during the initial HTTP connection will proceed over cleartext HTTP.

items.label: Mistake 2: add_header in Location Replacing Server Headers
items.content: This is the #1 mistake. Adding any add_header in a location block replaces ALL server-level add_header directives. The fix: either do not use add_header in locations, or repeat all headers.

items.label: Mistake 3: HSTS on HTTP Server Block
items.content: HSTS should ONLY be set on HTTPS server blocks. Setting it on an HTTP block does nothing (the browser discards it). More importantly, if you redirect HTTP→HTTPS via return 301, the HTTP response itself should NOT have HSTS — the browser would see HSTS on HTTP and ignore it.

items.label: Mistake 4: CSP Too Permissive (or Too Strict)
items.content: A CSP with 'unsafe-inline' on script-src provides minimal XSS protection. A CSP that does not include all your CDN domains will break your site. The fix: use Report-Only mode first, monitor violations, adjust, then enforce.

items.label: Mistake 5: Setting Headers at http Level and Then Using add_header in server
items.content: The same inheritance rule applies at every level. If you set headers at the http level and then use add_header in a server block, the server block replaces all http-level headers.

items.label: Mistake 6: Overlooking Server Header Information Disclosure
items.content: server_tokens off only removes the version number. The Server header still advertises 'nginx'. Some security standards require removing the Server header entirely, which requires third-party modules.

items.label: Scenario 1: Static Website
Scenario 2: API Backend
Scenario 3: Single Page Application (React/Vue/Angular)

sections.title: 17. Advanced: Additional Security Headers
sections.description: Less common but valuable security headers.

items.label: Cross-Origin Resource Policy (CORP)
items.content: Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy tells browsers which origins can read the response. This prevents side-channel attacks like Spectre by ensuring sensitive resources are not accessible from other origins.
items.code:
# Restrict to same-origin only (prevent Spectre attacks)
add_header Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy "same-origin" always;

# Other options:
# same-site — Allow same-site (including subdomains)
# cross-origin — Allow all

items.label: Cross-Origin Opener Policy (COOP)
items.content: Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy isolates your pages from cross-origin windows opened via window.open or target=_blank. This prevents cross-origin attacks on your window object.
items.code:
# Isolate your page from cross-origin openers
add_header Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy "same-origin-allow-popups" always;

# Other options:
# unsafe-none — Default, no isolation
# same-origin — Full isolation (can break OAuth popups)

items.label: Cross-Origin Embedder Policy (COEP)
items.content: Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy requires cross-origin resources to be loaded with CORS headers or be loaded through a CORP-compatible mechanism. This enables features like SharedArrayBuffer.
items.code:
# Require CORS for all cross-origin resources
add_header Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy "require-corp" always;

# WARNING: This can break CDN resources, images, and fonts
# that do not set CORS headers. Test thoroughly!

items.label: Cache-Control for Sensitive Pages
Expect-CT (Deprecated)
items.content: Expect-CT allowed sites to opt-into Certificate Transparency reporting. It is now deprecated in favor of built-in CT enforcement in modern browsers, but may still be useful for legacy environments.

sections.title: 18. Security Headers Checklist
sections.description: A printable checklist for deploying security headers.

rows: No add_header in location blocks that override server headers

items.label: Post-Deployment Verification
text: ✓ Run: curl -I https://yourdomain.com — check all headers are present
✓ Run: curl -I https://yourdomain.com/nonexistent — check headers on 404
✓ Visit: https://securityheaders.com — aim for A+ grade
✓ Visit: https://observatory.mozilla.org — aim for A+ grade
✓ Check browser DevTools → Network tab for every page type
✓ Verify HSTS preload eligibility at https://hstspreload.org (optional)
✓ Test CSP via Report-Only mode for at least 1 week
✓ Monitor logs for CSP violations and adjust policy accordingly

sections.title: 19. Troubleshooting Common Issues
sections.description: Fixing problems with security header deployment.

items.label: Issue: Headers Not Showing in Browser
items.content: Check: Did you reload NGINX? (sudo systemctl reload nginx). Check: Are you using add_header in a location block that overrides server-level headers? Check: Is the 'always' parameter missing on error pages?

items.label: Issue: Site Broken After Adding CSP
items.content: Your CSP is too restrictive. Switch to Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only mode and monitor violations. Each violation tells you exactly which resource was blocked and from which directive. Relax your policy to allow those resources.

items.label: Issue: HSTS Preload Submission Rejected
items.content: Common reasons: max-age < 31536000 (1 year), missing includeSubDomains, subdomains not serving HTTPS, redirect chain not fully HTTPS, or mixed content warnings on some pages.

items.label: Issue: Security Headers.com Grade is Low
items.content: Security Headers.com checks: presence of all major headers, HSTS with sufficient max-age, CSP with proper directives, and whether headers are present on the root page and error pages. Address missing items one at a time.

items.label: Issue: Headers Appear on Root Page But Not on Subpages
items.content: This is the add_header inheritance problem with near 100% certainty. Check if your subpage's location block uses add_header. If so, that location is replacing all server-level headers. See Section 3 for the fix.

sections.title: 20. Summary and Key Takeaways
sections.description: What you should remember from this guide.

items.label: Key Takeaways
text: ✓ Security headers are free, zero-cost protection against common web attacks — there is no reason not to implement them.
✓ The add_header inheritance trap is the #1 cause of missing headers in NGINX. Never use add_header in location blocks, or repeat all headers if you must.
✓ Always use the 'always' parameter on every security header to ensure error pages are protected.
✓ Start CSP in Report-Only mode, monitor violations, then enforce. A broken CSP breaks your site.
✓ Deploy HSTS gradually: test → 1 year → preload (if certain).
✓ Test your headers with curl, securityheaders.com, and Mozilla Observatory.
✓ server_tokens off is essential — never advertise your NGINX version.
✓ Use the included snippet (security-headers.conf) for consistent, maintainable configuration.

items.label: Production Quick Start
text: 1. Copy the security-headers.conf snippet to your server.
2. Include it in every server block.
3. Ensure no location block uses add_header.
4. Test with curl -I and securityheaders.com.
5. Aim for A+ grade on securityheaders.com.
